ation of jurisdictional issues has suggested a role for greater judicial
reliance upon constitutional principles, 435 and our analysis of substan-
tive antitrust issues has proposed that the courts should rely upon pur-
pose-based rules in order to mitigate the current uncertainty in
conventional doctrine. 43 6 The common element in these proposals is
their focus upon antitrust law as a set of standards that is designed
primarily to protect "individual rights" to fair treatment rather than to
promote "social policies" that serve the collective welfare. 4 37 This fo-
cus upon rights is obvious when constitutional values are implicated in
antitrust issues. The same focus also underlies our proposals for pur-
pose-based rules, however, in that these kinds of rules are well designed
to protect both the moral and legal rights of individuals to fair behavior
by others.
438
In the past several decades of American law, the prevailing ten-
dency among "legal realists" in general, and antitrust lawyers in partic-
ular, has been to view the law as the pursuit of policies or collective
social goals. 4 39 Two issues we have discussed are illustrative: the ap-
plication of a rigid per se rule against tie-in arrangements, 440 and the
invitation under the Rule of Reason to use an ad hoc balancing ap-
435. See supra text accompanying notes 99, 136-45, 151-97. Cf. supra text accompanying
notes 408-28 (a possible first amendment defense against antitrust attacks upon professional
credentialing activities).
436. See supra text accompanying notes 301-70.
437. On the important distinction between "rights" and "policies" in the adjudication of hard
cases, see Dworkin, Hard Cases, supra note 179, reprinted in R. DwoRxIN, TAKING RIGHTS SERI-
OUSLY 81 (1977). In brief, Dworkin argues that the making of social policy lies primarily in the
domain of legislatures and that judicial decisions in civil cases "characteristically are and should
be generated by principle not policy." Arguments of "policy" attempt to justify a government
decision by showing that it would serve the collective welfare, in general. Arguments of "princi-
ple" are arguments from standards that justify a government decision by showing that the decision
would secure "some individual or group right." Thus, when no settled rule disposes of a case, a
party may nevertheless have a right to win because of preexisting legal principles that support the
party's claim. See Dworkin, Hard Cases, supra note 179, at 1058-60, reprinted in R. DWORKIN,
TAKING RIGHTS SERIOUSLY 81-84.
438. A major instance of the connection between individual rights and purpose-based legal
analysis is, of course, the mens rea concept in criminal law. See H.L.A. HART, PUNISHMENT AND
RESPONSIBILITY (1968); cf. I. KANT, FOUNDATIONS OF THE METAPHYSICS OF MORALS 16-18 (L.
Beck trans. 1959) (fundamental morality, and fairness toward others, is obtained by acting in
accordance with duties that are specified by principles of universal applicability; thus, moral
worth and moral rights depend primarily upon "the principle of volition by which the act is done"
rather than upon the effects of actions).
439. On the history of legal realism and the realists' treatment of law as social policy, see
Woodard, The Limits of Legal Realism: An Historical Persfpective, 54 VA. L. REV. 689 (1968). See
also Summers, Pragmatic Instrumentalism in Twentieth Century American Legal Thought-A Syn-
thesis and Critique of Our Dominant General Theory About Law and Its Use, 66 CORNELL L. REv.
861 (1981). For good examples of the realist view of antitrust law, see R. BORK, supra note 12; C.
KAYSEN & D. TURNER, ANTITRUST POLICY (1959); R. POSNER, supra note 3.
440. See supra text accompanying notes 342-54.
CALIFORMA LAW REVIEW
[Vol. 70:595
proach in analyzing hospital privilege decisions."' At first glance,
these areas of doctrinal confusion and uncertainty appear to have radi-
cally different causes. The strict antitrust treatment of tie-ins results
from several Supreme Court decisions that were undertaken in the
"political" antitrust tradition of promoting a decentralized economy
and greater opportunities for small traders. 4 2 By contrast, the ten-
dency of antitrust courts to enage in ad hoc balancing under the Rule
of Reason has been substantially enhanced by the competing and now
dominant tradition of analyzing antitrust issues solely from the point of
view of economic efficiency and consumer welfare." 3
Both doctrinal problems, as well as the uncertainty on issues of
antitrust jurisdiction, may stem fundamentally from the same concep-
tual error-the judicial pursuit of social policies rather than concern for
individual rights that have been granted by constitutional and statutory
provisions. Thus, the "political antitrusters" have pursued interpreta-
tions of antitrust law that have focused upon restructuring the Ameri-
can economy and providing greater opportunities for small businesses
in general, rather than upon protecting the rights of individual consum-
ers and businesses to fair treatment. 4 " At the same time, "efficiency
analysis" in antitrust law has focused upon promoting consumer wel-
fare as a general policy, without particular regard for the rights of trad-
441. See supra note 3 and accompanying text; supra text accompanying notes 371-74; supra

442. See, eg., Fortner Enter. v. United States Steel Corp., 394 U.S. 495 (1969); Northern Pac.

(1947). For criticism of this approach, see R. POSNER, supra note 3, at 171-84.
443. On the substance of this "economic" tradition in antitrust law, see R. BORK, supra note
12; R. POSNER, supra note 3. On this tradition's apparent ascendancy at the current time, see
Continental T.V. v. GTE Sylvania, 433 U.S. 36 (1977); Williamson, Commentary, On the Polilcal
Economy ofAntitrust: Groundsfor Cautious Optimism in R. TOLLISON, supra note 20, at 77. To be
sure, the economic tradition of antitrust analysis does not logically require ad hoc judicial balanc-
ing as the preeminent analytic method, and in the hands of masters this tradition may indeed be
capable of generating categorical rules of some clarity and generality. See, e.g., R. POSNER, supra
note 3, at 171-211. Yet, if the ultimate purpose of antitrust law is efficiency, it may be unreasona-
ble to expect that lawyers and judges in general will hew to a few efficiency-based rules and avoid
the temptation to look at each case on its facts to determine which antitrust result will promote
efficiency in the particular case. This seems to be one of the practical dilemmas of any Rule
Utilitarianism, which instructs persons to follow rules for the purpose of achieving the greatest
utility even though a particular application of the rule may not have (or appear to have) the
desired result. See generally R. SARTORIUS, INDIVIDUAL CONDUCT AND SOCIAL NORMS 11-18
(1975).
444. See, eg., Blake & Jones, In Defense ofAntitrust, 65 COLUM. L. REV. 377 (1965). The
focus of the political antitrusters on policies of decentralizing the American economy and ex-
panding opportunities for small traders, at the expense of efficiency, has made this tradition a
relatively easy target for efficiency-minded analysts of antitrust law. This is because it can be
argued persuasively that antitrust law has not prevented the growth of big business by methods
that are beyond the reach of the antitrust laws and, furthermore, that encouraging monopolies
might be the best way to expand opportunities for small traders in general. The political antitrus-
ters' policies can thus be condemned as unrealistic, inconsistent, and perhaps counterproductive as
well. See, eg., R. POSNER, supra note 3, at 18-22.
ANTITRUST AND HOSPITAL PRIVILEGES
ers and consumers who have been wronged by the unfair acts of
economic enterprises. 445
Our final and most sweeping conclusion is thus that antitrust law
in general may benefit from some new thinking about the individual
rights that have been granted (or not granted) by Congress in its enact-
ment of the various antitrust statutes." 6 In particular, the Sherman Act
would seem to be a fruitful target for this rethinking. The extreme gen-
erality of the Act appears to invite the policy-laden analyses that have
produced so much apparent uncertainty and inefficiency in antitrust
decisionmaking. At the same time, there is evidence suggesting that
Congress primarily intended the Act to provide the federal courts with
jurisdiction to enforce the rights of traders and consumers to fair treat-
ment under the common law standards on restraints of trade and mo-
nopolies. 447 This is not to say that antitrust courts should try to freeze
the Sherman Act into a set of specific common law rules that may have
existed as of 1890, but to suggest that the legislative history of the Sher-
man Act would support a new antitrust focus upon individual rights.
One probable result of this focus, we suspect, would be an increasing
recognition of purpose-based antitrust rules to help specify fair and un-
fair economic behavior towards consumers and traders.
445. See, eg., R. BORK, supra note 12; R. POSNER, supra note 3.
446. Cf. Discussion" Public-Choice.Aspects ofAntitrust in THE POLITICAL ECONOMY OF ANTI-
TRUST, supra note 20, at 125-26 (discussion between Kenneth Dam and Victor Goldberg of a
"social property right" that legislatures and antitrust courts have recognized in situations in which
the property values of small businesses are adversely affected).
